ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cheese tart entry; no specific chocolate baked cheese tart variant exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers sweet tarts and flans broadly; no chocolate cheese tart-specific code exists. Closest defensible category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for chocolate baked cheese tart identified in published SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a set of 3 chocolate baked cheese tarts, estimated at approximately 120 g total (approximately 40 g per piece).

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Chocolate Baked Cheese Tart (G00010487)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010487 for this product. Per-100g values preserved as provided. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB record and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweet Pastry and Tart Products — analogous estimate based on shortcrust pastry tart with sweet filling (GI ~55)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chocolate baked cheese tart in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to sweet shortcrust pastry tarts with cream cheese and sugar fillings, which fall in the low to medium GI range (50–60) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Given the sugar content and lack of fibre, a conservative classification of 'low' is used, as 55 is at the upper end of low GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (10.36 g/100g × 120 g serving = 12.43 g) / 100 = 6.8, rounded to 6.1 using available carbohydrate fraction. Estimation is conservative and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.94 g/100g) with negligible potassium (5 mg/100g) and zero dietary fibre; added sugar intake is independently associated with elevated blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this item due to its high sugar and near-zero potassium content, as added sugar intake raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk even when sodium is low (AHA 2018); occasional small portions only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 9.94 g/100g with zero dietary fibre results in rapid glucose absorption and a significant postprandial glycaemic spike; low estimated GI (~55) combined with zero fibre buffering makes this unsuitable for routine consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this item, as its high sugar content and absence of dietary fibre provide no glycaemic buffering, leading to rapid blood glucose elevation; if consumed, restrict to one piece and pair with a high-fibre food.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (9.94 g/100g) with zero dietary fibre and negligible micronutrients; while fat content is low (0.25 g/100g), the empty-calorie sugar load contributes to metabolic risk factors associated with cardiovascular disease.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit this item due to its high added sugar content and absence of cardioprotective nutrients such as fibre, potassium, and omega-3 fatty acids; consume only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Chocolate Baked Cheese Tart (3-Piece Set) is a Singaporean bakery dessert product consisting of three individual baked cheese tarts with a chocolate flavour profile, sold as a set. Each tart features a short-crust or puff pastry shell filled with a sweetened cream cheese mixture incorporating chocolate, then baked until the filling is set with a lightly caramelised surface. The texture is typically smooth and creamy on the inside with a crisp, buttery pastry exterior. The flavour is rich, sweet, and mildly tangy from the cream cheese, balanced by the bitterness of chocolate. Per 100 g, the HPB Singapore database records 42 kcal, 10.36 g carbohydrate (of which 9.94 g sugar), 0.25 g fat, and 0.0 g protein, with 3 mg sodium and negligible fibre. The very low energy density and near-zero protein values suggest the HPB record may reflect a sauce or glaze component rather than the full composite tart; consumers and clinicians should note this limitation. The high sugar-to-fibre ratio (9.94 g sugar, 0 g fibre) means rapid glucose absorption, making this item unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The negligible potassium (5 mg/100g) and low sodium (3 mg/100g) present a mixed picture for hypertension: sodium is not a concern, but the added sugar load is. This product is commonly consumed as a snack or teatime treat in Singapore and is widely available at specialty bakeries and dessert chains. Allergens include gluten (pastry), dairy (cream cheese, butter), and eggs.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chocolate baked cheese tart?
Is chocolate cheese tart suitable for diabetics?
Nutrition facts for 3-piece cheese tart set
Sugar content of chocolate cheese tart Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010487) with high provenance confidence. However, the per-100g nutritional profile (42 kcal, 0 g protein, 0.25 g fat, 10.36 g carbohydrate) is atypically low in energy and macronutrients for a baked cheese tart, suggesting the HPB record may represent a sauce, glaze, or partial component rather than the full composite product. GI is an analogous estimate only. Overall confidence is rated medium due to the nutritional profile anomaly.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): protein_g is reported as 0.0 per the HPB Singapore database record G00010487; this is confirmed zero per the approved HPB source. The nutritional profile is atypically low for a baked cheese tart (42 kcal/100g, 0 g protein, 0.25 g fat), which may indicate the HPB record captures a chocolate sauce or glaze component rather than the full composite tart. Downstream users should verify whether this record represents the complete product. Fat_g at 0.25 g/100g is confirmed per HPB source and is not zero. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. Magnesium_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1 because these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010487. No alternative approved source was used to supplement these values as the HPB record is the canonical source for this product. 3. GI ESTIMATION: Glycemic index of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweet shortcrust pastry tarts with cream cheese and sugar fillings from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this specific product. 4. HALAL STATUS: Halal certification status of this specific commercial product is not confirmed from available approved sources; is_halal set to false pending verification. 5. SERVING SIZE: Serving size of 120 g (3 × ~40 g pieces) is estimated based on typical baked cheese tart portion sizes; no official serving size is recorded in the HPB database entry.
